speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-oh. We better stop.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape four of an interview with Mr. Aharon Golub. My name is Judith Ehrenfeld. Mr. Golub, you were telling me, when we ended, about the house that you were-- the place you were living for six months before the Russians liberated you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Finally the construction of the big log that I told you that we dug it into our mountain, like one way, was completed. And we moved in there. And I mentioned to you that before long, there was a house full of people, that my aunt could not refuse for various reasons. And we lived in that place fairly-- I mean, it was very crowded. But I mean, it was-- we felt safe, because my uncle was there. And his son was there. We had the weapons. And we were fairly secluded into an area away from major, major passageways near a swamp on one hand and a mountain on the other hand. And food was already-- we, at least we had enough food at that point. So-- and we heard news from time to time that the Germans are retreating and, and the Russians are coming. And, and was more frequent activities of the, the Russian partisans. They were more visible. And we heard the various actions they were taking. So our hopes, our hopes were raised again that perhaps somehow we're going to make it through the war. It was-- in, in comparison to what I lived through prior to that, living in this house was like being on vacation already, being in a resort place, because there was food. We had a big oven. We could bake our own bread. And we had meat. And we had-- we ate reasonably well. And we had enough food, because we lived in the forest. So I would say this was already the better part of, of, of the war. And in February of 1944, we were liberated by the Russians. Uh, Ludwipol was the, the, the county seat, so to speak, the center town, where the government used to have all, all their buildings to administrate the entire area, with all the villages that I mentioned to you before. Before the Russians left the city prior to the German entry, they burned down the city completely, because they felt that there were too many-- too-- it would-- that would help the Germans a lot, if the city was not left intact. As a result, the town was burned down, and, and, and they had to find another place. And they had selected a village called Moczulanka. It's a, it's a Russian name. And in that, in that little village, they, they made a county seat. As a result, we-- my uncle went into that area. And he managed to obtain-- to rent a room for us, a big room in a, in a farmer's house. And we all moved into this village. And we were already free citizens. The Russians came in, and they established a government. And, of course, the Germans had retreated. And sort of we returned to be normal human, human beings at that point. We lived in this village for about six months, approximately. And at that point, the Russians had made a declaration that everyone that was a Polish citizen prior to the war has an option either to remain in the territory or to be repatriated deeper into Poland, because this area was going to be annexed by Russia as part of the war bounty, and it no longer going to belong to Poland. The border was moved further up. So in line of Jewish tradition always to move to the west, we decided that we would be repatriated. And we lived in-- we moved-- I don't know if I mentioned to you that we moved from the village to, to Rowne, prior, prior to our repatriation. I'm sorry. I should have mentioned that. The sequence is a little wrong. From the village, we decided to move to a bigger city called Rowne. And my uncle, my uncle managed to get us a, a an apartment in the city. And we moved there by horse and wagon. We went from, from our town to another town called [NON-ENGLISH]. And from [NON-ENGLISH], there was already a highway, a road, going to Rowne. From there, we managed to get to-- to rent a truck. And they took us, whatever meager possessions we owned, to Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your aunt and your uncle and your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: --my uncle, my aunt, myself, and my cousin. And my other cousin, was-- he returned back from the-- from the partisans, and he got a government job in the village that we lived in, the provisional village. And he remained there for the time being. We lived in Rowne. For six months, from February till August.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do when you lived there? How did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- it was a big city. Prior to the war, there were 60,000 Jewish people in that city. After the war, there was very few left. But there was still-- when we arrived there, there were many refugees that-- from the surrounding, surrounding, surrounding area. And there was two synagogues that were functioning. And little by little, we-- people found friends and relatives. And there was not too bad a life in the city itself. My uncle had got a-- had a government job. And he used to work for a government insurance company. And we did everything possible to earn money. Even I, myself, used to sell cigarettes on the street. And, and my aunt used to cook her wonderful Jewish soup in [? pail ?] quantities. And we used to bring it to the market. Every Monday there was a farmer's market. And all the farmers used to come into the big city to sell their produce. So there was a demand for food. So we used to sell soup and rolls. And we earned money that way. The Russians did not exactly approve of private enterprise. But small-scale initiatives, they did not exactly-- did not pursue vigorously. So for a time-- for a while, we were, we were doing that. And I did not go to school. There were no schools at the time. And I helped, I used to help my uncle. I used to ride on a bicycle. And sometimes I used to help them, help my aunt bring the food to the market. And we just sort of waited. We knew about the repatriation. My uncle was making arrangements for us to go deeper into Poland. And after living in Rowne for a, I would say approximately six months or so, we left in the beginning of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the winter? January or February?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was-- perhaps it was in March. I don't really recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was sort of-- it was not that cold already. And they provided a train for us. The Russians provided the train. And all the people that were registered were supposed to showed up at a specific time. And each one was assigned half a car, or you know, whatever, based on their family size. And at that point, my, my other cousin, that worked for the Russian government, had met a woman that he was interested in. And she came along with us also. So we were already like, sort of an extended family. And again, there was some single young man that didn't have anybody. And my uncle took him along with us. And it was a very long journey, because we were going on one of the Russian trains. And it was a supplementary type train. So every time when major trains were coming, we were put on a side, on a side railing. Sometimes we waited two, three days till we got permission to move again. But slowly, surely we arrived to a city called [? Beuthen. ?] This city was-- belonged to German, to Germany, prior to the war. But when they readjusted the borders, when Ukraine was given back to the Russians, a portion of Germany was given to the Poles as compensation for the land they ceded to, to the Soviet Union. We came to [? Beuthen. ?] And the town was full of Jewish refugees coming from all over the place. My uncle managed to rent an apartment. And he did a little business. I don't know exactly what it was doing exactly-- was buying and selling things. And it was very fashionable. People used to travel to Germany, because after the war, they were also devastated. And for food, they used to exchange clothes. They would bring food to Germany and in return get personal possessions, watches and clothes. So it was that type of business going on. And a lot of people were doing business that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the war wasn't officially over still.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was over where we were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the war still continued further out, deep into Germany. But in our area, for all intents and purposes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the war was over. And I discovered that they have Jewish youth organizations in town. And I had made up my mind that I will never live sort of in the Diaspora any longer at the time. And I wanted to go only to Israel. And my family wanted to go to the United States. My uncle, my uncle and my aunt and their two sons wanted to go to the United States. [BACKGROUND NOISE] I decided that I have-- that I will separate from them at that point, because I made up my mind that I will only go to Israel. And I joined the-- it was sort of like an orphanage home, but it was ran by, by Israelis from-- it was actually ran by people, survivors, from, from the uprising in the, in the Warsaw ghetto. But they were all connected with Israeli organizations. And the sole purpose of this home was to gather single children-- you know, orphans-- and, and, and try to bring them to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I joined this place. And I was a little older than the average child. I was, at that time, approximately, 18 years old, or 17-- close to 18. And I spoke Hebrew fluently. So immediately, I was [? up in ?] classes and we started to teach the children Hebrew. And I liked this life very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at the time, meanwhile, the war ended. And as you know, the British were, were controlling Palestine at the time. Was a British mandate. And because of oil interests and political interests, they went along with the Arabs as to limit the immigration of Jews to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was only 1,500 permits that the British government used to issue per month. Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but since the group that I belonged to was mostly younger children, and, and most of them orphans, we were able to get-- to go to Israel in a legal manner. You know, we got British authorization to go to Israel. So we did not go on any of the illegal ships that you know, you're probably familiar with, that used to be eventually taken to Cyprus, that the British used to turn many people back from the Israeli shores. And a few months later, at that time, it was already the beginning of 1946. And I think in March of 1946, we were told that we got permission to go to Israel. And we were all put on a train. And we went to Marseilles, in France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know-- by a train. It was a special train. I think the Israeli organizations, together with the-- maybe with the UNRA, with the Joint Distribution Committee, I suppose. Everybody was into things. They got a special train. It took us two weeks to travel. But it was already very pleasant, and plenty food. And we felt safe. And we arrived to Marseilles. In Marseilles, we were put into a provisional camp for about 10 days till the boat. And we were checked out, you know, medically and so forth. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And your feet? How were your feet? You were checked out [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My feet were-- I was-- I did, I did not have any special shoes. I had regular shoes with a lot of cotton. And I mean, I was in pain all the time. And I struggled. But I was young. And I was-- it felt like I was coming to life again. And I felt like I was a flower in April, and I'm blooming again. So who pays attention to a little pain, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were brought to Marseilles. And the boat arrived one of those days. And the name of the boat was And we boarded the French ship, you know. And, and in April 26, 1946, we arrived to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived to Israel, we were-- since we came as legal residents, we were taking into a camp for a week or so, for screening and so forth. And they issued us passports and documentation. And we were assigned, the whole group, to go to-- to live on a kibbutz. The Jewish agency made arrangements for us to go to Kibbutz Yagur, near Haifa, very prominent, major kibbutz. And we were put on buses and brought to the kibbutz. And the entire kibbutz was waiting for us. They left all their work. And there was a very major celebration. And they practically dragged us out of the bus to the dining room. And they had a major reception for us. And, and, and from that point on, our life had changed completely. We were assigned to homes, nice, nice rooms. And we were given clean clothes. And we were taken for medical checkups. And it was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a kibbutz for eight years. For eight years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I must tell you, this is the-- was actually the best years of my life. The people extended themselves so much for us. Especially, it's interesting to know that the girls had lice in their, in their hair. And the woman, which was assigned to, to take care of us, she used to come in the middle of the night and wake up the girls and clean their-- and wash their hair with kerosene, or who knows what she used at the time, because she didn't want the boys to, to make fun out of them during the day. You know? So unbelievable devotion. And we were assigned to-- we went to school. Half a day we went to school. And a half a day, we went to work. And the Jewish agency paid for us, paid the kibbutz whatever medical expenses. And after, after being in the kibbutz for a little while, they, they examined my feet. And they decided to do some surgery. So they brought me to a beautiful hospital in Haifa on the Carmel. And they did-- they removed certain sections in my foot to make it possible for the skin to grow, sort of closer to each other, and-- because there was a lot of scar tissue. And, and they provided me with orthopedic shoes. And my life was, was improved considerably.

speaker: interviewee
text: Since, since I could speak Hebrew, and I was a little older than the rest of them, so I was put in a position of management of the group. And I was sort of their leader for a while. You know? All the presentations and knowing the language, sort of put me in a position of leadership.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we, we were approximately 40 children that we came to the kibbutz. And some single kids were added on to us from various-- the Jewish agency used to evaluate, when a single child appeared from some location or he lived with older people, they attached him to us. And we were-- in the kibbutz, we were trained to go and make our own settlements. The purpose of our being in the kibbutz, they called this [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] was a preparatory step for, for creating our own settlement at some location, which was strategically important. So each one of us was assigned jobs in various areas of the kibbutz, so we would have total knowledge how to run the kibbutz. Myself, since I had difficulties with my feet, they, they wanted me to, to work in the management, in the financial management of the kibbutz. But prior to that, they sent me for an IQ test, to see if, if I'm able to do that. And I passed the test satisfactory. I was-- they were satisfied with the results. And as a result, they sent me to a business school. It's a college, a business college, in Haifa, for two years. And I was working in the, in the management of the kibbutz. And as a bookkeeper, I worked in, in, in the office of the kibbutz. But I was very unhappy, because my friends worked in the field. And I used to look clean, and they looked dirty. So I felt bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So after negotiating with the kibbutz for a while, they let me work during the harvest time in the agriculture. And I-- to prove, to prove my capabilities, my friends taught me quietly how to drive the tractors and the truck. And when they finally took me out on a test, I-- they didn't have to show me anything, because I, I knew, I knew what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I was-- we lived there from '46, till just prior to the War of Independence in 1948. We were trained secretly, because the British were still controlling Israel, to use various type of weapons. And, and we were taught judo and whatever it, whatever it requires. And then the entire group, we joined the, the Haganah. And not only did we join the Haganah, we joined the commando group, which used to be called the Palmach. This was like the crown jewel of the Jewish defense forces. And they had a policy of taking the whole group, the girls and the boys. And you were not separated, because the purpose of us being together was, to go and to-- after the war, to go and to create a settlement. So, so they, they were very careful not to break up the, the unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all lived in the military barracks with the, with the girls. And the girls were also in the army. But even myself, that I could not do certain duty in the field, I could drive a jeep. And I could do other military tasks and so forth. So.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the War of Independence, the Palmach was disband, because Israel already had a regular military force. And the group as a whole returned back to the kibbutz, except for some special-- some of our guys that became specialists in certain military areas. They remained sort of almost on loan to the army, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: But not you? You went back to the kibbutz?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I returned back with the most of the group--

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to the, to the kibbutz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, we, we, we were, we were put together with other groups, because in order to have a settlement, you needed at least the equivalent of 120 people. So we made, we made acquaintance and friends with other groups. And I think in 1949, the beginning of 1949, the-- our group went to the Negev, in the-- in, in, sort of in the south of Israel, near a city, near Be'er Sheva, south of Be'er Sheva. And we established a settlement. I still remained-- I had-- did not conclude yet my school. So I remained in Kibbutz Yagur till I finished my school. And then I owed them, sort of, for my sustenance, a certain amount of days. So I remained there for a while, in the kibbutz, to pay my debts. So I worked full, full days to repay them for, for all the support and the food and, and the living quarters they gave me while I was going to school. And at that point, they, they, they assigned me a job to, to, to-- the kibbutz-- all the kibbutzim had, like, a central management that was in Tel Aviv, that sort of helped all, all of them. It was like sort of a little-- like a little government of the kibbutzim, that any kind of problems that they had, they would call and they would seek help.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was an organization that-- our, our organization had 48 kibbutzim. It was a very major organization. And they had-- the management of the kibbutz was in Tel Aviv. And what did you have to do with that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They assigned me a job to be a driver, because we had our own little, like, a ministry of transportation, because we needed-- we had 30 cars to serve the entire organization, because every time there was a problem, someone qualified had to go to assist the people with their problem. It was-- at that time, there was still a military problem, was safety problems. It was a, it was a sort of a little self-sustaining organization. So I was assigned to be a driver. And after a short period of time, the man that ran this, the 30-- the man in charge of the transportation became sick and he had to resign from his position. So they appointed me to be in charge of the group.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I lived-- we-- the kibbutz used to rent rooms for us in Tel Aviv. And we used to stay there only five days a week. And Friday, we would go back to the kibbutz. So I worked at this job till October, practically till before I left for the United States. In, in, in between my family here, I discovered that I have here a grandfather and two of my father's brothers, uncles. And one of the uncles lived in Argentina.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by this time, my aunt and my uncle that saved my life had moved to Canada. So they, they wanted me very much to come and live in the United States. And I had refused to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at some point, we agree that I will come for a visit. So on October 1926, I came to visit. 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry. In October 24, 1954, I came to the United States. And while I was here visiting, I met my lovely wife Ruth. And we got married. And I decided to stay in the United States. So that's sort of part, part of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you interested to know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what happened after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We, we-- I, I got a job. And I was always gainfully employed all my life. I never was unemployed here for a single day. My wife also worked for a while. But when we had-- we have-- we had two children. We have two children. And when we had the kids, she stopped working. I have a son, which is 38 years old. And I have a daughter, which is 33 years old. My son is a graduate from MIT. He has a PhD in applied economics, and a very successful young man. He's very much involved in Jewish affairs. He's a major contributor to UJA. He went to Israel during the time of the Scud missiles to, to, to live with this people. And he thought he wanted to feel this on his own, on his own skin.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: The burden. My daughter is a graduate from Oswego College. And she's a paralegal. And she works in the legal profession. She has a very nice job. She's not married as of this moment. And I'm very proud of both of them. We have three-- have three grandchildren. Two of them are three years old, and the baby is 10 months old. And my son married a very nice young lady, very educated. She also had a very high position. So I'm very proud of our accomplishments.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop you now, Mr. Golub.
